title: Sampling-Aware Ancestral State Inference
text: vignette demonstrates use saasi package ancestral state reconstruction. SAASI (Sampling-Aware Ancestral State Inference) ancestral state reconstruction method accounts variation sampling rates among locations traits. Unlike traditional methods assume uniform sampling, SAASI explicitly models heterogeneous sampling, leading ancestral state estimates take sampling account. SAASI described Song et al. (2025).

title: Sampling-Aware Ancestral State Inference
text: can install development version saasi GitHub :
code: # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("MAGPIE-SFU/saasi")

title: Sampling-Aware Ancestral State Inference
text: saasi function requires three main inputs: phylogenetic tree (class phylo) : Rooted binary branch lengths units time (positive) Contains tip states (tree$tip.state) missing values SAASI function prepare_tree_for_saasi() can ensure conditions met. Transition rate matrix Q (class matrix): Rates transition states Use estimate_transition_rates() estimate matrix. Birth-death-sampling parameters (class data.frame): Speciation rate Extinction/removal rate Sampling rate Use estimate_bds_parameters() estimate speciation rate single, overall sampling rate. “extinction rate” (language birth-death models) infectious disease applications inverse duration infectiousness, .e. rate leaving infectious class. output data frame containing probability state internal node phylogenetic tree.

previous_headings: Example: Ebola 2013-2016 West African Ebola Epidemic
title: Sampling-Aware Ancestral State Inference
text: example, use data Nextstrain (Hadfield et al. 2018; Sagulenko, Puller, Neher 2018): https://nextstrain.org/ebola/ebov-2013?c=country. First, create data frame containing strains states. first column data frame match tree$tip.label, second column contain state interest. Users can skip step tree$tip.state already exists.
code: tree <- ape::read.tree(system.file("extdata", "nexstrain_ebola_ebov-2013_smaller.nwk", package = "saasi")) metadata <- readr::read_tsv(system.file("extdata", "nextstrain_ebola_ebov-2013_metadata.tsv", package = "saasi")) #> Rows: 1493 Columns: 8 #> ── Column specification ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Delimiter: "\t" #> chr  (7): strain, country, division, author, author__url, accession, accessi... #> date (1): date #>  #> ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data. #> ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message. tip_data <- data.frame(   tip_label = metadata$strain,   state = metadata$country )

previous_headings: Example: Ebola 2013-2016 West African Ebola Epidemic
what: Modify the tree to be compatible with saasi
title: Sampling-Aware Ancestral State Inference
text: Users can apply function prepare_tree_for_saasi() make tree compatible saasi. check tree compatible, use function check_tree_compatibility(). , function include tip states (.e. location) tree tree downsampled version 2013 Ebola virus tree nextstrain. 193 tips three countries.
code: ebola_tree <- prepare_tree_for_saasi(tree, tip_data) plot_saasi(ebola_tree, saasi_result = NULL, tip_cex = 1, res = 900)

previous_headings: Example: Ebola 2013-2016 West African Ebola Epidemic
title: Sampling-Aware Ancestral State Inference
text: Users can estimate transition rate matrix Q using function estimate_transition_rates(). Users can specify model transition rate matrix: 1. Equal rate ER, 2. Symmetric rate SYM, 3. rates different ARD, custom structure matrix.
code: Q <- estimate_transition_rates(ebola_tree, method = 'fitMk', matrix_structure = 'SYM')

previous_headings: Example: Ebola 2013-2016 West African Ebola Epidemic
title: Sampling-Aware Ancestral State Inference
text: Users can estimate speciation sampling rates using function estimate_bds_parameters(). infectious disease applications, users knowledge expected removal rate (1/μ1/\mu) disease interest, saasi requires extinction rate input (overcome identifiability limitations). obtain estimates overall speciation sampling rates, users knowledge maximum minimum R0, well upper lower bounds total removal rate (1/(μ+ψ))(1/(\mu+\psi)). Ebola, assume total infectious period (1/(μ+ψ))(1/(\mu+\psi)) 20 40 days. Converting years, total removal rate (μ+ψ\mu + \psi) 9.125 (365/40) 18.25 (365/20). assume μ=5\mu = 5, gives us bounds overall sampling rate ψ\psi 4.125 13.25. example, assume R0 1.5 3. Now set state-specific sampling pass parameters SAASI. overall sampling rate estimated (comparable λ\lambda μ\mu); SAASI allows us account state-specific relative sampling. Suppose, example, Liberia known sequenced smaller fraction cases states, factor 2.
code: rates <- estimate_bds_parameters(     ebola_tree,     mu = 5,     r0_max = 3,     r0_min = 1.5,     psi_max = 15,     infectious_period_min = 20/365, # convert days to years     infectious_period_max = 40/365, # convert days to years     n_starts = 100)

previous_headings: Example: Ebola 2013-2016 West African Ebola Epidemic
title: Sampling-Aware Ancestral State Inference
code: pars = data.frame(state = colnames(Q), lambda=rates$lambda, mu = rates$mu, psi= rates$psi, row.names = NULL) pars[(pars$state=="Liberia"),"psi"] = pars[(pars$state=="Liberia"), "psi"]/2 # set sampling rate to be 1/2 the baseline

previous_headings: Example: Ebola 2013-2016 West African Ebola Epidemic
title: Sampling-Aware Ancestral State Inference
code: saasi_ebola <- saasi(ebola_tree, Q, pars) #> Tree is compatible with SAASI

previous_headings: Example: Ebola 2013-2016 West African Ebola Epidemic
title: Sampling-Aware Ancestral State Inference
text: Users can use built-function plot_saasi() visualize results.
code: p1 <- plot_saasi(ebola_tree, saasi_ebola, tip_cex = 1, node_cex = 0.5, res = 900)

title: Sampling-Aware Ancestral State Inference
text: states comparable sampling obtain different ancestral state reconstruction:
code: pars = data.frame(state = colnames(Q), lambda=rates$lambda, mu = rates$mu, psi= rates$psi, row.names = NULL) saasi_ebola2 <- saasi(ebola_tree, Q, pars) #> Tree is compatible with SAASI  p2 <- plot_saasi(ebola_tree, saasi_ebola2, tip_cex = 1, node_cex = 0.5, res = 900)

previous_headings: Example: Ebola 2013-2016 West African Ebola Epidemic
title: Sampling-Aware Ancestral State Inference
text: saasi() function returns data frame probability distributions states internal node. Higher probabilities indicate greater confidence ancestral state. example, assume Liberia sampled lower rate two countries, see internal nodes inferred Liberia sampling locations. SAASI accounts undetected cases Liberia. Note Liberia factor 2 chosen entirely random purposes vignette represent knowledge opinion authors relative sampling outbreak.

title: SAASI Simulation Study
text: vignette demonstrates accuracy SAASI (Sampling-Aware Ancestral State Inference) simulation studies. simulate phylogenetic trees known ancestral states using birth-death-sampling process, assess well SAASI can recover internal states.

title: SAASI Simulation Study
text: Load required packages:
code: library(saasi)

title: SAASI Simulation Study
text: use 3-state model heterogeneous sampling rates test SAASI’s ability handle sampling bias: Key feature: State 1 sampling rate 0.1, states 2 3 sampling rates 1.0. creates sampling bias SAASI designed handle.
code: # Define birth-death-sampling parameters demo_pars <- data.frame(   state = c("1", "2", "3"),   root_prior = c(1/3, 1/3, 1/3),   lambda = c(3, 1.5, 1.5),        # Birth rate, also varies across states   mu = c(0.1, 0.1, 0.1),          # Death rate   psi = c(0.1, 1.0, 1.0)          # Sampling rate (heterogeneous!) )  # Define transition rate matrix Q demo_Q <- matrix(0.3, 3, 3) diag(demo_Q) <- -0.6 rownames(demo_Q) <- c("1", "2", "3") colnames(demo_Q) <- c("1", "2", "3")

title: SAASI Simulation Study
text: First, let’s demonstrate single simulation understand workflow: Extract true ancestral states (ground truth): Run SAASI infer ancestral states: Calculate accuracy:
code: set.seed(123)  # Generate tree with built-in post-processing phy <- sim_bds_tree(   params_df = demo_pars,   q_matrix = demo_Q,   x0 = 1,                    # Start at state 1   max_taxa = 500,            # Initial tree size   max_t = 50,                # Maximum time depth   min_tip = 100              # Minimum tips after post-processing ) # The true internal node states are stored in node.state true_states <- phy$node.state # Run SAASI  saasi_result <- saasi(phy = phy,        # phylogenetic tree                       Q = demo_Q,       # transition rate matrix                       pars = demo_pars) predicted_states <- apply(saasi_result, 1, which.max)  # Calculate accuracy accuracy <- mean(predicted_states == true_states) cat("Accuracy for this simulation:", round(accuracy, 3), "\n") #> Accuracy for this simulation: 0.9

title: SAASI Simulation Study
text: contrast, standard tools designed account differences sampling. Inferring ancestral states ace: Let’s plot truth, ace predictions saasi predictions:  SAASI much closer truth ace, accounts lower sampling state 1 (red). consequence lower sampling (course) red tips less well-represented tree process created tree. Even observed tips states 2 3, internal nodes giving rise might well state 1. SAASI correctly estimates many red internal nodes top bottom portions tree, whereas ace’s simpler discrete trait phylogeography model . SAASI’s likelihood computation accounts fact clade red ancestor still likely quite green blue tips , since sampled higher rate red tips.
code: ace_result = ace(phy$tip.state, phy, type="d") ace_predictions = apply(ace_result$lik.anc, 1, which.max) true_result = 0*saasi_result for (k in 1:nrow(true_result)) { true_result[k, true_states[k]] = 1 } op <- par(mfrow = c(1, 3), mar = c(1, 1, 3, 1), oma = c(0, 0, 0, 0)) on.exit(par(op), add = TRUE) plot_saasi(phy, true_result, tip_cex = 1, node_cex = 1, res = 900); title("Truth") plot_saasi(phy, saasi_result, tip_cex = 1, node_cex = 1, res = 900); title("SAASI") plot_saasi(phy, ace_result$lik.anc, tip_cex = 1, node_cex = 1, res = 900); title("ACE")

what: Check if phylogenetic tree is compatible with saasi — check_tree_compatibility
title: Check if phylogenetic tree is compatible with saasi — check_tree_compatibility
text: Verifies compatibility phylo object saasi() function without modifying object. tree compatible saasi(), must: rooted, correct number internal nodes (number tips - 1), unary nodes polytomies, positive branch lengths, valid tip state annotations tips. tree compatible saasi(), user can run prepare_tree_for_saasi() resolve issues.

title: Check if phylogenetic tree is compatible with saasi — check_tree_compatibility
code: check_tree_compatibility(tree)

title: Check if phylogenetic tree is compatible with saasi — check_tree_compatibility
text: tree object class phylo.

title: Check if phylogenetic tree is compatible with saasi — check_tree_compatibility
text: Logical. Returns TRUE tree compatible saasi FALSE otherwise.

title: Check if phylogenetic tree is compatible with saasi — check_tree_compatibility
code: # Check if this demo tree is compatible with saasi: check_tree_compatibility(demo_tree) #> Tree is not compatible with SAASI #> NA tip states present. Remove with drop_tips_by_state(tree, NA) #> No tip states. Attach with attach_tip_states() or prepare_tree_for_saasi() #> [1] FALSE  # Now check another tree which has been properly prepared: check_tree_compatibility(demo_tree_prepared) #> Tree is compatible with SAASI #> [1] TRUE

title: Drop tips from a phylogenetic tree by their state values — drop_tips_by_state
code: # This demo tree has 13 tips in 3 possible states demo_tree_prepared #>  #> Phylogenetic tree with 13 tips and 12 internal nodes. #>  #> Tip labels: #>   sp2, sp4, sp8, sp9, sp10, sp26, ... #> Node labels: #>   nd1, nd2, nd6, nd22, nd27, nd74, ... #>  #> Rooted; includes branch length(s). table(demo_tree_prepared$tip.state) #>  #> 1 2 3  #> 2 6 5   # Remove all tips in state 3 new_tree <- drop_tips_by_state(demo_tree_prepared, "3")  # The resulting tree has 8 tips, having removed all tips in state 3 new_tree #>  #> Phylogenetic tree with 8 tips and 7 internal nodes. #>  #> Tip labels: #>   sp2, sp4, sp8, sp9, sp26, sp30, ... #> Node labels: #>   nd1, nd2, nd6, nd22, nd27, nd32, ... #>  #> Rooted; includes branch length(s). table(new_tree$tip.state) #>  #> 1 2  #> 2 6

what: Estimate rate parameters of a birth-death-sampling process — estimate_bds_parameters
title: Estimate rate parameters of a birth-death-sampling process — estimate_bds_parameters
text: estimate_bds_parameters() estimates speciation rate \(\lambda\) sampling rate \(\psi\) birth-death-sampling process given phylogenetic tree death rate \(\mu\). function uses user-specified epidemiological constraints sampling rate, basic reproduction number, duration infectious period improve estimability.

title: Estimate rate parameters of a birth-death-sampling process — estimate_bds_parameters
code: estimate_bds_parameters(   phy,   mu,   trim = c(0.1, 0.5),   n_starts = 100,   force_two_step = FALSE,   psi_max = 7,   r0_min = 1,   r0_max = 5,   infectious_period_min = NULL,   infectious_period_max = NULL )

title: Estimate rate parameters of a birth-death-sampling process — estimate_bds_parameters
text: phy object class phylo branch lengths. Must rooted binary. mu extinction rate. must non-negative. trim numeric vector length 2 specifying quantiles removing node times lineages--time regression (see Details). Default value c(0.10, 0.50). n_starts number starting points multi-start optimization. Default value 100. force_two_step Logical. TRUE, uses two-step estimates even violate constraints (see Details). Default value FALSE. psi_max Maximum sampling rate per unit time. Must strictly positive. Default value 7. r0_min Minimum basic reproductive number (\(R_0 = \lambda/(\mu+\psi)\)). Must strictly positive. Default value 1. r0_max Maximum basic reproductive number. Must strictly positive. Default value 5. infectious_period_min Minimum infectious period (\(1/(\mu+\psi)\)). Must strictly positive. Default value NULL. infectious_period_max Maximum infectious period (\(1/(\mu+\psi)\)). Must strictly positive. Default value NULL.

title: Estimate rate parameters of a birth-death-sampling process — estimate_bds_parameters
text: list following attributes: lambda: Estimated speciation rate psi: Estimated sampling rate mu: Input death rate r0: Estimated basic reproductive number (\(\lambda/(\mu+\psi)\)) infectious_period: Estimated infectious period (\(1/(\mu+\psi)\)) net_diversification: Estimated net diversification rate (\(\lambda-\mu-\psi\))

title: Estimate rate parameters of a birth-death-sampling process — estimate_bds_parameters
text: Estimation carried first estimating reproduction number \(\frac{\lambda}{\mu+\psi}\) net diversification rate \(\lambda-\mu-\psi\), solving unknown \(\lambda\) \(\psi\). net diversification rate estimated performing lineages--time (LTT) regression node times within quantiles specified trim input reproduction number estimated first computing maximum likelihood estimates (MLEs) \(\lambda\) \(\psi\) numerically optimizing likelihood compute reproduction number. stability MLEs assessed checking estimate \(R_0\) within 0.02 1. estimates \(\lambda\) \(\psi\) satisfy constraints specified psi_max, r0_min, r0_max, infectious_period_min, infectious_period_max parameters, MLEs \(\lambda\) \(\psi\) returned, unless force_two_step=TRUE case original estimates \(\lambda\) \(\psi\) returned regardless whether constraints satisfied. LTT regression fails MLEs \(\lambda\) \(\psi\) returned. MLEs obtained numerically using L-BFGS-S algorithm (see optim implementation details) n_starts times randomly chosen starting points selecting best maximizer attempts. Due user-imposed constraints, randomly generated starting points valid, 100*n_starts attempts made generate valid starting points. may result fewer n_starts optimizations actually performed. two-step method produces estimates violate constraints numerically unstable, function falls back MLE estimates step 1.

title: Estimate rate parameters of a birth-death-sampling process — estimate_bds_parameters
code: # Use the demo tree with bounds based on the demo parameters # - Removal rate of 0.3 # - Infectious periods range from 0.75 to 1.5 years # - An upper bound on the sampling rate of 1 # - Plausible values for the basic reproduction number are between 1.5 and 2 estimate_bds_parameters(demo_tree, mu = 0.3,                                    psi_max = 1,                                    infectious_period_min = 0.75,                                    infectious_period_max = 1.5,                                    r0_min = 1.5,                                    r0_max = 2) #> $lambda #> [1] 2.510424 #>  #> $psi #> [1] 0.9546599 #>  #> $mu #> [1] 0.3 #>  #> $r0 #> [1] 2.00088 #>  #> $infectious_period #> [1] 0.7970287 #>  #> $net_diversification #> [1] 1.844612 #>

what: Estimate the transition rate matrix of the discrete trait process — estimate_transition_rates
title: Estimate the transition rate matrix of the discrete trait process — estimate_transition_rates
text: estimate_transition_rates() computes maximum likelihood estimate transition rate matrix \(Q\) governing discrete trait process. discrete traits evolve according Markov process transition rate matrix \(Q\) element \(q_{ij}\) instantaneous rate transitioning state \(\) state \(j\).

title: Estimate the transition rate matrix of the discrete trait process — estimate_transition_rates
code: estimate_transition_rates(tree, matrix_structure = "ER", method = "ace")

title: Estimate the transition rate matrix of the discrete trait process — estimate_transition_rates
text: tree phylo object tip.state attribute assigning traits tips. matrix_structure form transition rate matrix. Can either numeric matrix one following strings: "ER", "SYM", "ARD" (see details). default value "ER". method method used perform estimation. Possible values "ace" "fitMk".

title: Estimate the transition rate matrix of the discrete trait process — estimate_transition_rates
text: transition rate matrix fit observed phylogeny. matrix compatible saasi functions.

title: Estimate the transition rate matrix of the discrete trait process — estimate_transition_rates
text: model parameters controls structure \(Q\). "ER" specifies equal rates transitions, \(q_{ij}=q\) traits \(,j\). "SYM" specifies symmetric transition rate matrix, \(q_{ij}=q_{ji}\) traits \(,j\). "ARD" places structural constraints allows traits different, \(q_{ij}\ne q_{kl}\) traits \(,j,k,l\). another grouping transition rates desired can input numeric matrix entry matrix positive integer entries value share rate. method parameter allows user select preferred method ace fitMk, however preferred method fails used instead. ace selected, maximum likelihood estimator computed using ape::ace() function. fitMk selected, phytools::fitMk() function used fit \(Q\) provided phylogenetic tree.

title: Estimate the transition rate matrix of the discrete trait process — estimate_transition_rates
code: # Use the demo tree demo_tree #>  #> Phylogenetic tree with 13 tips and 12 internal nodes. #>  #> Tip labels: #>   sp2, sp4, sp8, sp9, sp10, sp26, ... #> Node labels: #>   nd1, nd2, nd6, nd22, nd27, nd74, ... #>  #> Rooted; includes branch length(s).  # Use the simmap function to estimate the rate transition matrix # - Impose equal rates for the matrix structure estimate_transition_rates(demo_tree_prepared, matrix_structure = "ER") #>            1          2          3 #> 1 -1.7548671  0.8774336  0.8774336 #> 2  0.8774336 -1.7548671  0.8774336 #> 3  0.8774336  0.8774336 -1.7548671  if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Use ace to estimate the rate transition matrix # - Impose a structure such that transitions to or from the first state #   happen at a different rate than second and third states struct <- matrix(c(0, 1, 2,                    1, 0, 2,                    2, 2, 0), nrow=3, ncol=3) estimate_transition_rates(demo_tree_prepared, matrix_structure = struct) } # }

what: Plot a phylogenetic tree with SAASI tip annotations — plot_saasi

title: Plot a phylogenetic tree with SAASI tip annotations — plot_saasi
code: plot_saasi(   tree,   saasi_result,   colours = NULL,   tip_cex = 0.5,   node_cex = 0.2,   save_file = NULL,   width = 3000,   height = 3000,   res = 300 )

title: Plot a phylogenetic tree with SAASI tip annotations — plot_saasi
text: tree object class phylo tip.state attribute. saasi_result Output saasi() containing internal node annotations. colours Optional character vector specifying node colour state. default, colours automatically generated based number states. tip_cex Size tips. Default value 0.5. node_cex Size node pie charts. Default value 0.2. save_file Optional string specifying file path plot saved. default plot displayed current device. width Width plot pixels. relevant saving file. Default value 3000 pixels. height Height plot pixels. relevant saving file. Default value 3000 pixels. res Plot resolution dpi. relevant saving file. Default value 300 dpi.

title: Plot a phylogenetic tree with SAASI tip annotations — plot_saasi
text: save file specified, plot saved directed. , plot displayed users device. function returns output plot.phylo(), list containing plot specifications.

title: Plot a phylogenetic tree with SAASI tip annotations — plot_saasi
code: # Run SAASI saasi_res <- saasi(demo_tree_prepared, demo_Q, demo_pars) #> Tree is compatible with SAASI  # Plot the results using the default settings plot_saasi(demo_tree_prepared, saasi_res)   if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Use custom colours and save the result to tree.png plot_saasi(tree, saasi_result,            colours = viridis::viridis(3, begin=0.1, end=0.9),            node_cex = 0.3,            save_file = "tree.png") } # }

what: Reformat a phylogenetic tree to be compatible with saasi — prepare_tree_for_saasi
title: Reformat a phylogenetic tree to be compatible with saasi — prepare_tree_for_saasi
text: prepare_tree_for_saasi() take phylo object reformat output compabtible saasi function. reformatting includes: Adding tip annotations Resolving polytomies Imposing minimum branch length Removing undesired states

title: Reformat a phylogenetic tree to be compatible with saasi — prepare_tree_for_saasi
code: prepare_tree_for_saasi(   tree,   tip_data = NULL,   resolve_polytomies = TRUE,   fix_branches = TRUE,   min_branch_length = 1e-05,   drop_states = character(0) )

title: Reformat a phylogenetic tree to be compatible with saasi — prepare_tree_for_saasi
text: tree object class phylo. tip_data Either named vector data.frame. named vector, elements vector must state tip names must labels tips. data.frame, must 2 columns: first tip labels second tip states. can omitted tree already tip.states attribute. resolve_polytomies Boolean. Indicates whether polytomies resolved. Default value TRUE. Setting FALSE produce tree incompatible saasi. fix_branches Boolean. Indicates whether zero negative branch lengths fixed. Default value TRUE. Setting FALSE produce tree incompatible saasi. min_branch_length Numeric. minimum branch length resultant tree. short branch lengths increased accordingly. Default value 1e-5. drop_states optional character vector states remove tree.

title: Reformat a phylogenetic tree to be compatible with saasi — prepare_tree_for_saasi
text: object class phylo compatible saasi() function.

title: Reformat a phylogenetic tree to be compatible with saasi — prepare_tree_for_saasi
text: reformatting conducted follows: Polytomies resolved using ape::multi2di(). length branches zero negative length set min_branch_length. tips NA value state annotation removed addition states specified drop_states.

title: Reformat a phylogenetic tree to be compatible with saasi — prepare_tree_for_saasi
code: # Check if the demo tree is compatible check_tree_compatibility(demo_tree) #> Tree is not compatible with SAASI #> NA tip states present. Remove with drop_tips_by_state(tree, NA) #> No tip states. Attach with attach_tip_states() or prepare_tree_for_saasi() #> [1] FALSE  # Since tip annotations are missing, we need state data for the tips head(demo_metadata) #>   node state #> 1  sp2     1 #> 2  sp4     2 #> 3  sp8     2 #> 4  sp9     1 #> 5 sp10     3 #> 6 sp26     2  # Reformat the demo tree with the tip states tree_prepared <- prepare_tree_for_saasi(demo_tree, demo_metadata)  # Check the compability of the reformatted tree check_tree_compatibility(tree_prepared) #> Tree is compatible with SAASI #> [1] TRUE

title: Sampling-Aware Ancestral State Inference — saasi
text: Reconstructs ancestral states internal nodes phylogenetic tree accounting heterogeneous sampling rates across states locations.

title: Sampling-Aware Ancestral State Inference — saasi
code: saasi(phy, Q, pars, sensitivity_test = FALSE)

title: Sampling-Aware Ancestral State Inference — saasi
text: phy object class phylo specifying phylogenetic tree. tree must rooted, binary, contain tip.state attribute. Use check_tree_compatibility() function verify phy satisfies conditions. Q \(n\times n\) transition rate matrix, \(n\) number states. Row column names must correspond states. -diagonal elements transition rates states; diagonal elements set rows sum zero. pars data.frame parameters used ancestral state reconstruction algorithm. Must following columns: state: vector state names. match column row names Q. lambda: vector birth rates birth-death-sampling process. mu: vector death rates birth-death-sampling process. psi: vector sampling rates birth-death-sampling process. can also root_prior specifies priori distribution state root; one provided uniform distribution root state used. sensitivity_test boolean indicating sensitivity test conducted (see Details). default value FALSE.

title: Sampling-Aware Ancestral State Inference — saasi
text: data.frame state probabilities internal node phy.

title: Sampling-Aware Ancestral State Inference — saasi
text: sensitivity_test=TRUE, test sensitivity saasi birth-death-sampling rate parameters conducted. values \(\lambda\), \(\mu\), \(\psi\) perturbed sampling values random +/- 10% input value. ancestral state reconstruction run perturbed parameters; repeated 10 times. summarise sensitivity, modal state internal node first computed perturbed reconstruction. total number differences modal states computed perturbed reconstructions original reconstruction. average number total differences across 10 perturbed reconstructions reported.

title: Sampling-Aware Ancestral State Inference — saasi

what: Simulate a phylogenetic tree from a birth-death-sampling process — sim_bds_tree
title: Simulate a phylogenetic tree from a birth-death-sampling process — sim_bds_tree
text: sim_bds_tree() simulates phylogenetic tree state annotations internal nodes tips. birth-death-sampling process used generate tree topology branch lengths; include speciation, sampling, mutation events. continuous-time Markov process used assign states nodes. resulting tree compatible saasi(). tree post-processed remove tips present ensure minimum number tips.

title: Simulate a phylogenetic tree from a birth-death-sampling process — sim_bds_tree
code: sim_bds_tree(   params_df,   q_matrix,   x0,   max_taxa = 100,   max_t = 100,   include_extinct = FALSE,   min_tip = 1 )

title: Simulate a phylogenetic tree from a birth-death-sampling process — sim_bds_tree
text: params_df data.frame specifying parameters tree simulated. Must contain lambda: vector speciation rates mu: vector extinction rates psi: vector sampling rates number rows equal number states. q_matrix State transition rate matrix. x0 root state. max_taxa Maximum number nodes allowed initial simulated tree. Default value 100. max_t Maximum depth allowed returned tree. Default value 100. include_extinct Boolean. TRUE, extinct taxa included returned tree. Default value FALSE. min_tip Minimum number tips required post-processed tree. processed tree fewer tips, simulation repeated condition satisfied. Default value 1.

title: Simulate a phylogenetic tree from a birth-death-sampling process — sim_bds_tree
text: object class phylo compatible saasi().

title: Simulate a phylogenetic tree from a birth-death-sampling process — sim_bds_tree
code: # Simulate a tree from the demo model with root state set to 1 demo_pars #>   state rootprior lambda  mu psi #> 1     1 0.3333333    1.5 0.3 0.1 #> 2     2 0.3333333    1.5 0.3 0.5 #> 3     3 0.3333333    1.5 0.3 0.5 demo_Q #>      1    2    3 #> 1 -0.6  0.3  0.3 #> 2  0.3 -0.6  0.3 #> 3  0.3  0.3 -0.6 sim_bds_tree(demo_pars, demo_Q, 1) #>  #> Phylogenetic tree with 37 tips and 36 internal nodes. #>  #> Tip labels: #>   sp1, sp2, sp3, sp4, sp5, sp6, ... #> Node labels: #>   nd1, nd2, nd9, nd14, nd19, nd20, ... #>  #> Rooted; includes branch length(s).
